key_insight: A fully differentiable, GPU-accelerated FEM framework can simulate finite-strain anisotropic plasticity and compute accurate inverse-design gradients without hand-derived sensitivities.
engineering_relevance: This enables faster, more scalable calibration of complex constitutive models for advanced manufacturing, especially when experiments are expensive or limited.
method: Automatic differentiation through constitutive update and solver
Inverse characterization using heterogeneous, topology-optimized specimens with full-field displacement data
evidence_signal: On a 3-million-DOF forward problem, a single NVIDIA H100 achieved up to 9.4× speed-up over a 24-core CPU Abaqus baseline, and AD gradients outperformed finite differences in accuracy and cost.
watch_item: Performance and robustness likely depend on GPU memory, sparse solver implementation, and the difficulty of extending the workflow to highly non-smooth or poorly identified material models.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
